content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

February 25, 1991


Joan R. Mihay
785 Cabrillo Place
Morro Bay, CA 93442

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
    Our File No. I-90-766

Dear Ms. Mihay:

     This is in response to your letter of December 10, 1990,
requesting advice regarding the use of surplus funds and a ballot
measure committee's reporting obligations under the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").1 Because your request does not involve a
pending governmental decision, we will provide you with informal
assistance.2

QUESTIONS

1. What may a candidate do with leftover campaign funds?

2. When may a ballot measure committee file a termination
report?

CONCLUSIONS

     1. Generally, a successful candidate may use his or her
campaign funds for any expenditure that is reasonably related to a
legislative, governmental, or political purpose. However, in some
circumstances the expenditure must be directly related to a
legislative, governmental or political purpose. Donations to bona
fide nonprofit organizations are permissible if reasonably related
to a legislative, governmental or political purpose. In addition,
the candidate may save the money for his or her next election and
transfer the money to the new campaign account.

_______________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
  are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
  regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
  18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
  Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
  immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No  I-90-766
Page 2


     An unsuccessful candidate may only use surplus funds for
specified purposes, including payment of outstanding campaign
debts, pro rata repayment of contributions, donations to bona fide
nonprofit organizations, contributions to a political party or
committee, and contributions to support or oppose any candidate
for federal office, out-of-state office, or any ballot measure.

     2. A ballot measure committee may file a statement of
termination at any time, so long as (1) it has ceased to receive
contributions and make expenditures and does not anticipate
receiving contributions or making expenditures in the future; (2)
it has eliminated all debts or declared that it has no intention
or ability to discharge all of its debts; (3) it has no surplus
funds, and (4) it has filed all required campaign statements
disclosing all reportable transactions, including the disposition
of surplus funds.

                              FACTS

     You are the treasurer for a city council candidate and a
ballot initiative committee. You would like to know the options
for the candidate's use of leftover campaign funds, and whether he
can save the funds for his next campaign or give them to a non-
profit organization which is succeeding the initiative committee.
You also want to know whether the ending date for purposes of
filing a termination report on behalf of the ballot measure
committee would be the election day.

                            ANALYSIS

     The permissible uses for funds remaining in a campaign
account after the election depend on whether the candidate was
successful in the election.

     A successful candidate may use the funds for any expenditure
that is reasonably related to a legislative, governmental, or
political purpose. If the expenditure confers a "substantial
personal benefit," i.e., a direct personal benefit with a value of
more than $100 to the elected officer, the expenditure must be
directly related to a legislative, governmental, or political
purpose. (Sections 89512.) Please review Sections 89513 through
89518 for details on specific expenditures.

     Saving the funds for the next election campaign is reasonably
related to a political purpose and therefore a permissible use of
the funds, if a candidate was successful. Please note, however,
that the candidate must transfer the funds to the new bank account
when he opens the account for the new campaign. (Section 85201.)


_________________________
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

Our File No  I-90-766
Page 3

A successful candidate may make a donation or loan to
nonprofit organizations under Section 89515:

Campaign funds may be used to make
donations or loans to bona fide charitable,
educational, civic, religious, or similar tax-
exempt, nonprofit organizations, where no
substantial part of the proceeds will have a
material financial effect on the candidate,
elected officer, member of his or her
immediate family, or the campaign treasurer,
and where the donation or loan bears a
reasonable relation to a political
legislative, or governmental purpose.

You have not provided enough information about the nonprofit
organization to allow us to determine whether it meets the
standard enunciated in Section 89515.

An unsuccessful candidate is limited in how he or she may use
surplus funds.  Section 89519 provides:

Upon leaving any elected office, or at
the end of the postelection reporting period
following the defeat of a candidate for
elective office, whichever occurs last,
campaign funds raised after January 1, 1989,
under the control of the former candidate or
elected officer shall be considered surplus
campaign funds and shall be disclosed pursuant
to Chapter 4 (commencing with Section 84100)
and shall be used only for the following
purposes:

(a)  The payment of outstanding campaign
debts or elected officer's expenses.

(b)  The pro rata repayment of
contributions.

(c)  Donations to any bona fide
charitable, educational, civic, religious or
similar tax-exempt, nonprofit organization,
where no substantial part of the proceeds will
have a material financial effect on the former
candidate or elected officer, any member of
his or her immediate family, or his or her
campaign treasurer.

(d)  Contributions to a political party
or committee so long as the funds are not used
to make contributions in support of or
opposition to a candidate for elective office.

Our File No  I-90-766
Page 4


     (e) Contributions to support or oppose
any candidate for federal office, any
candidate for elective office in a state other
than California, or any ballot measure.

     Thus under Section 89519, the candidate may make a donation
to a nonprofit organization under the same conditions as a
successful candidate, except that the unsuccessful candidate need
not demonstrate that the donation is reasonably related to a
legislative, governmental, or political purpose. Again, we do not
have enough information to determine whether the nonprofit
organization meets the standard set forth above.

     An unsuccessful candidate may not save the surplus funds for
another election because that is not one of the permissible uses
listed in Section 89519.

     A ballot measure committee may terminate its status as a
committee, and thus avoid further filing obligations only by
filing a Statement of Termination declaring, under penalty of
perjury, that the candidate or committee:

          (1) Has ceased to receive contributions and make
expenditures and does not anticipate receiving contributions
or making expenditures in the future;

          (2) Has eliminated or has declared that it has no
intention or ability to discharge all of its debts, loans
received and other obligations.

          (3) Has no surplus funds; and

          (4) Has filed all required campaign statements
disclosing all reportable transactions.

                    Regulation 18404, copy enclosed.

     Thus if the committee wishes to terminate its status as a
committee, it must first dispose of its leftover funds. Donating
them to a nonprofit organization is permitted. It must file a
semi-annual statement (either Form 420 or Form 450) to report the
disposition of leftover funds and any other reportable
transactions. Thereafter it may file a Form 415, Statement of
Termination, if the four criteria listed above in Regulation 18404
are met.

Our File No  I-90-766
Page 5

I trust this letter adequately addresses your questions.  If
you have any further questions regarding this matter, please feel
free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

Peggy Bernardy (15)
By:  Peggy Bernardy
     Counsel, Legal Division

SH:PB:dg

Enclosure
